Population Growth Calculator

Projected Population
—
Population Increase

Doubling Time

howItWorks.intro: Enter a starting population, a growth rate per period, and the number of periods. The calculator compounds the rate forward with P(t) = P₀ × (1 + r)^t, then applies the rule of 70 to estimate how long the count takes to double.
rules: Rule of 70: Doubling time ≈ 70 ÷ Growth Rate %
howItWorks.outro: The exponential model assumes the growth rate holds steady across every period. Real populations shift as birth rates, migration, and resource limits change, so treat a 20-year projection as a trend line, not a guaranteed count.

faq.question: What is the difference between discrete and continuous growth?
faq.answer: Discrete growth, P = P₀ × (1 + r)^t, compounds once per period and is the right fit for annual census counts or year-by-year city planning. Continuous growth, P = P₀ × e^(rt), compounds every instant and better models bacteria or organisms that reproduce constantly. At small rates the two nearly agree: a 2% rate over 10 years gives 1.02^10 ≈ 1.219 discretely versus e^(0.2) ≈ 1.221 continuously — a gap under 0.2%. The difference widens as the rate or the time span grows.

faq.question: What is the Rule of 70?
faq.answer: Divide 70 by the growth rate as a whole-number percent to estimate how many periods a population takes to double. A town growing 2% a year doubles in about 70 ÷ 2 = 35 years; at 1% it takes roughly 70 years; a bacterial culture growing 7% an hour doubles in about 10 hours. The 70 comes from the natural log of 2 (≈ 0.693) scaled to percent, so it is an approximation that stays accurate for rates under about 10%.

faq.question: How do I find the growth rate from two censuses?
faq.answer: Divide the later count by the earlier count, take the t-th root where t is the number of periods between them, and subtract 1. If a county held 80,000 in 2010 and 96,000 in 2020, that is r = (96,000 ÷ 80,000)^(1/10) − 1 = 1.2^0.1 − 1 ≈ 0.0184, or about 1.84% a year. Enter that rate back into the calculator to project the next decade.

faq.question: Is exponential growth realistic for long projections?
faq.answer: For 10 to 20 years the constant-rate model tracks most towns and countries closely. Over longer spans growth usually slows as housing, food, and jobs cap the count — the logistic (S-curve) model captures that ceiling better. Many developed nations already sit near zero or negative natural growth, so a fixed positive rate will overstate their long-run numbers.

faq.question: Can I project a declining population?
faq.answer: Yes. Enter a negative growth rate. A city of 700,000 shrinking 1.5% a year reaches 700,000 × 0.985^10 ≈ 601,700 in a decade. The rule of 70 still applies in reverse: at −1.5% the population halves in roughly 70 ÷ 1.5 ≈ 47 years.

faq.question: What growth rates are typical?
faq.answer: As of the mid-2020s the world population grows about 0.9% a year, the United States about 0.5%, and fast-expanding metros like Austin, Boise, or Raleigh 2–4%. Bacterial cultures in ideal conditions can exceed 100% per hour. Pick a rate that matches the population you are modeling rather than a global average.

howWeCalculate.intro: The calculator runs the discrete exponential formula and the rule of 70 on the numbers you enter, all in your browser.
steps: Read the current population as P₀, the annual growth rate as a decimal r (2% becomes 0.02), and the years to project as t
Compute the projected population P(t) = P₀ × (1 + r)^t — for 500,000 at 1.5% over 20 years that is 500,000 × 1.015^20 ≈ 673,900
Subtract P₀ from P(t) to report the population increase — about 173,900 in that example
Estimate doubling time with 70 ÷ (rate as a percent) — 70 ÷ 1.5 ≈ 47 years at that rate
howWeCalculate.disclaimer: These are estimates that assume a constant growth rate. For binding demographic or planning decisions, verify against official census projections and local birth, death, and migration data.

whenToUse.intro: Reach for this calculator whenever you have a starting count and a growth rate and need to see where the number lands.
scenarios: A demographer or planner projecting a town or country forward a decade to size schools, water, and transit
A biology student modeling a bacterial or animal population and comparing discrete to continuous growth
A city council estimating when a metro will double so infrastructure is funded before demand arrives
A researcher deriving a growth rate from two census counts to compare regions
A teacher demonstrating the rule of 70 and exponential curves with concrete numbers in class

sections.title: What Is the Population Growth Calculator?
sections.body: This calculator projects how a population changes when it grows or shrinks at a steady rate. Enter the current headcount, an annual growth rate, and a time span, and it applies the discrete exponential formula P = P₀ × (1 + r)^t. A town of 50,000 growing 2% a year reaches 50,000 × 1.02^10 ≈ 60,950 in a decade, and by the rule of 70 it doubles in about 70 ÷ 2 = 35 years. It works the same for a country, a city, or a species in a biology lab.

sections.title: Discrete Versus Continuous Growth
sections.body: Two models describe exponential growth. The discrete form, P = P₀ × (1 + r)^t, compounds once per period and suits yearly census data. The continuous form, P = P₀ × e^(rt), compounds every instant and fits organisms that reproduce without pause. At low rates they nearly match — 2% over 10 years gives a factor of 1.02^10 ≈ 1.219 discretely versus e^(0.2) ≈ 1.221 continuously — but the gap widens as the rate or the number of periods climbs, which is why the model you pick matters for long projections.

sections.title: Getting the Growth Rate From Two Counts
sections.body: If you know a population at two points in time, you can back out its rate. Divide the later count by the earlier one, take the root for the number of periods, and subtract 1: r = (P_later ÷ P_earlier)^(1/t) − 1. A city that went from 120,000 in 2015 to 138,000 in 2025 grew at (138,000 ÷ 120,000)^(1/10) − 1 ≈ 1.41% a year. Feed that rate back in to project the next decade, or use the rule of 70 to see it would take about 70 ÷ 1.41 ≈ 50 years to double.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Current Population
howToSteps.description: Type the starting headcount as P₀ — the latest census figure, a town's current residents, or the size of a lab culture.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Annual Growth Rate (%)
howToSteps.description: Enter the growth rate per period as a percent, such as 1.5 for 1.5% a year. Use a negative number for a shrinking population.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Years to Project
howToSteps.description: Set how many periods to compound forward. Ten to twenty years is where the constant-rate model tracks reality most closely.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Run the numbers to apply P = P₀ × (1 + r)^t and the rule of 70. Results appear instantly below.

howToSteps.title: Review Your Results
howToSteps.description: Read the projected population, the total increase, and the doubling time. Rerun with a higher or lower rate to bracket a best- and worst-case scenario.

tips: Convert the percent to a decimal in your head to sanity-check the output: 2% means multiplying by 1.02 each year, so 3 years is 1.02 × 1.02 × 1.02 ≈ 1.061.
Use the rule of 70 as a fast gut check — if the doubling time the calculator shows is far from 70 ÷ your rate, re-examine the inputs.
For a lab organism that reproduces continuously, remember the discrete model here slightly understates the count; the continuous e^(rt) form runs a hair higher.
Bracket uncertainty by projecting with a low, medium, and high rate rather than trusting a single number 20 years out.
When deriving a rate from two censuses, match the exponent to the gap between them — a 5-year gap uses a 1/5 root, not 1/10.
